title: Gromov-Witten Invariants and Complex Cobordism
speaker: Shaoyun Bai
abstract: Since the beginning of the subject, it has been speculated that Gromov-Witten invariants should admit refinements in complex cobordism. I will propose a resolution of this question based on joint work-in-progress with Abouzaid, building on recent advances in Symplectic Topology (FOP perturbations developed jointly with Xu) and functorial resolution of singularities from algebraic geometry.

proof_stages.label: Ordinary complex cobordism of stably complex manifolds
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines stable complex structures, the complex cobordism group over a point, its ring structure, and recalls the Thom-Milnor computation of the complex cobordism ring.

proof_stages.label: Thom-Pontryagin model via bundles and sections
proof_stages.summary: The geometric cobordism problem is replaced by homotopy groups of Thom spaces/spectra, where a map gives a complex vector bundle with a lifted section whose transverse zero set recovers a stably complex manifold.

proof_stages.label: Equivariant transversality obstruction
proof_stages.summary: After putting finite group actions into the Thom-Pontryagin picture, equivariant sections generally cannot be made transverse, so the appropriate replacement is to keep the ambient space, obstruction bundle, and section as part of the object.

proof_stages.label: Derived orbifold cobordism with obstruction bundles
proof_stages.summary: The derived cobordism group is modeled by orbifolds equipped with complex obstruction bundles and sections with compact zero locus, modulo cobordism, localization near the zero locus, and stabilization.

proof_stages.label: Gromov-Witten moduli as derived cobordism classes
proof_stages.summary: Moduli spaces of stable J-holomorphic maps naturally define invariant classes in the derived cobordism group, because automorphisms and nontransverse Cauchy-Riemann sections prevent them from directly giving ordinary cobordism classes.

proof_stages.label: Splitting theorem and symplectic consequences
proof_stages.summary: The inclusion of ordinary complex cobordism into the derived theory admits a splitting, compatible with equivariant transfers, so Gromov-Witten classes can be pushed to MU and then to any complex-oriented cohomology theory, with applications to Hamiltonian loop actions.

proof_stages.label: Geometric construction of the splitting
proof_stages.summary: The splitting is constructed by measuring and removing the nonsmoothness of derived zero loci using complex polynomial-like perturbations, functorial embedded resolution of singularities, and orbifold blow-ups that make quotient spaces smooth while leaving genuine manifolds unchanged.

examples.name: Equivariant real-line map with sign representation
examples.note: The Z/2-equivariant map from a trivially acted-on real line to the sign representation must be the zero map, exhibiting why equivariant transversality can fail and motivating retention of bundle-section data.
examples.engagement_reason: he analyzes the Z/2-equivariant map between real lines with trivial and sign actions, concluding only the zero map exists and checking transversality fails

passes.reason: the forced zero C2-equivariant map from a trivial real line to a sign line is a collapsed map used to demonstrate failure of equivariant transversality

examples.name: The finite group C2 in equivariant cobordism
examples.note: In response to a question about canonicity, the speaker cites Gamma=C2 as a case where equivariant cobordism can be computed explicitly and many splittings can be written down, illustrating non-canonicity.
examples.engagement_reason: C2 is cited as a finite group in equivariant cobordism for which explicit calculations are possible, but no such calculation is performed

examples.name: Z/2 quotient of sign and trivial-plus-sign representations
examples.note: The comparison of C with sign action, whose quotient is singular, to C_triv direct-sum C_sign, whose quotient is smooth, shows why making local characters independent removes orbifold quotient singularities.
